name: Book of Revelation
shortDescription: The Book of Revelation is the final book of the New Testament, containing apocalyptic visions and prophecies concerning the end times, the second coming of Christ, and the establishment of a new heaven and new earth.
description: The Book of Revelation, also known as the Apocalypse of John, is a complex and symbolic text within Christian scripture. It is traditionally attributed to John of Patmos, who wrote down his visions while exiled on the island. The book is characterized by its use of vivid imagery, symbolic language, and prophetic pronouncements about the end of the world as we know it. Key themes include the struggle between good and evil, the ultimate triumph of God and Christ over Satan and his forces, the judgment of humanity, and the promise of salvation for the righteous. Revelation draws heavily on Old Testament prophetic traditions, particularly those found in the books of Daniel, Ezekiel, and Isaiah. The book's apocalyptic nature has made it a source of both inspiration and controversy throughout Christian history. Interpretations vary widely, ranging from literal readings that see the events as imminent to more allegorical approaches that understand the book as a symbolic representation of spiritual warfare and the ongoing battle between faith and doubt. Its enduring influence is evident in art, literature, and popular culture, where its imagery of beasts, angels, plagues, and the final judgment continues to resonate. The book provides a culminating vision of divine justice and the ultimate restoration of harmony and peace in a renewed creation, offering hope and encouragement to believers facing persecution and hardship. Comparisons can be drawn between Revelation and other apocalyptic narratives across different mythologies, such as the Norse Ragnarok, which depicts a cataclysmic battle leading to the destruction and rebirth of the world, or Hindu traditions surrounding the avatar Kalki, foretelling the end of the Kali Yuga and the ushering in of a new, golden age. The cyclical destruction and renewal motif is a common thread across these narratives.

sources.title: The Encyclopedia of Apocalypticism, Volume 1: The Origins of Apocalypticism in Judaism and Christianity
sources.author: John J. Collins
